message.reasoning_content:
We need to create second library that depends on first library and calls its public function.

Current repository structure:

library-chain/core/... (core library)

Now we need to add library-chain/feature (project) that depends on core library.

We need to create go.mod for feature module, maybe module example.com/feature, and a file that imports example.com/core and calls core.Hello.

Also maybe a main.go to run.

The validators:

- feature-references-core: failure_class=api_contract
- feature-builds: failure_class=build

Thus we need to ensure that feature module imports core correctly and builds.

We need to include go.mod for feature, and a source file (maybe feature.go) that calls core.Hello.

Probably also a main.go to run.

But the validator "feature-references-core" likely checks that the feature library references core's API correctly (i.e., imports core and uses Hello). So we need to have a public function in feature that calls core.Hello.

Thus create library-chain/feature/feature.go with package feature, function Greet(name string) string that returns core.Hello(name). Also maybe a main.go to demonstrate.

Also need go.mod for feature: module example.com/feature, require example.com/core v0.0.0 (or replace). Since core is local, we can use replace directive.

In Go modules, to refer to local module, we can add replace example.com/core => ../core (relative path). So go.mod:

module example.com/feature

go 1.22

require example.com/core v0.0.0

replace example.com/core => ./../core

But relative path from feature directory to core: "../core". So replace example.com/core => ../core

Thus feature can import "example.com/core".

Now feature.go:

package feature

import "example.com/core"

func Greet(name string) string {
    return core.Hello(name)
}

Optionally add a main.go to call feature.Greet.

But not required for validator; but could be helpful.

Better include main.go:

package main

import (
    "fmt"
    "example.com/feature"
)

func main() {
    fmt.Println(feature.Greet("World"))
}

But need to import feature module; we need go.mod for feature includes module example.com/feature, and replace for core.

Thus we need to add files:

library-chain/feature/go.mod
library-chain/feature/feature.go
library-chain/feature/main.go (optional)

The validator "feature-builds" will try to build the feature module; maybe they run "go build ./..." in feature directory. So having a main.go ensures building a binary; but they might just run "go test" or "go build". Having a package with no main may still build (go build will build packages). But to be safe, include a main.go.

Thus output JSON with these three files.

Now ensure content formatting.

go.mod content:

module example.com/feature

go 1.22

require example.com/core v0.0.0

replace example.com/core => ../core

Make sure newline at end.

feature.go content:

package feature

import "example.com/core"

func Greet(name string) string {
    return core.Hello(name)
}

main.go content:

package main

import (
    "fmt"
    "example.com/feature"
)

func main() {
    fmt.Println(feature.Greet("World"))
}

Now produce JSON.
